chunks.title: Cardio Basics for Beginners
chunks.text:
Provide low-risk cardio guidance for beginner plans that need simple intensity
control.

- Low to moderate intensity is the default starting point for deconditioned
  users.
- A conversational pace is usually a safer default than maximal interval work.
- Session difficulty should match the user's training history, available time,
  and recovery capacity.

- Walking
- Cycling
- Elliptical training
- Marching in place
- Low-impact circuits with short work periods

- Short sessions can start around 10 to 20 minutes and extend over time.
- Cardio can be standalone or placed after strength work when fatigue does not
  compromise safety.
- Intervals for beginners should use generous recovery periods and avoid
  all-out sprint language.

- Stop sessions for chest pain, severe breathlessness, faintness, or unusual
  symptoms.
- Low-impact options are preferred when joint irritation is already present.

chunks.title: Beginner Training Principles
chunks.text:
Provide stable beginner guidance that helps the planner choose a manageable
weekly structure instead of an aggressive plan.

- Most beginners respond well to 2 to 4 training days per week.
- Full-body training is usually simpler to recover from than high-volume split
  routines.
- Start with moderate effort and leave a small performance buffer instead of
  pushing every set to failure.
- Use a mix of squat, hinge, push, pull, carry, and core patterns across the
  week when equipment allows.
- Increase difficulty gradually through small jumps in reps, time, load, or
  session density.

- Choose fewer exercises done consistently rather than many exercises rotated
  randomly.
- Match session length to the user's actual schedule so adherence stays realistic.
- Beginners benefit from repeating the same main patterns long enough to learn
  technique and confidence.
- Rest days should be spread across the week when training frequency increases.

chunks.title: Beginner Training Principles
chunks.text:
- Sharp pain, dizziness, chest pain, or severe shortness of breath are stop
  signals, not adaptation signals.
- Existing injuries or movement limitations should reduce exercise complexity and
  range of motion demands.

chunks.title: Warm-Up and Cool-Down Basics
chunks.text:
Support plan sections that prepare users for training and end sessions gradually.

- Warm-ups should raise body temperature and rehearse the session's movement
  patterns.
- Five to ten minutes is often enough for beginner sessions.
- Dynamic movements are usually a better fit before training than long passive
  stretching blocks.

- Brisk walking
- Marching in place
- Arm circles
- Bodyweight squats to a comfortable depth
- Light band rows or shoulder activation drills

- Cool-downs can gradually reduce effort and reinforce comfortable range of motion.
- Easy walking and gentle stretching are usually sufficient for beginner plans.
- Cool-downs should not introduce painful or advanced mobility drills.

chunks.title: Recovery, Sleep, and Rest Days
chunks.text:
Keep generated plans realistic by treating recovery as part of the program, not
as an optional extra.

- Beginners usually need at least one full rest day each week.
- Poor sleep, persistent soreness, and declining motivation can signal that
  total workload is too high.
- Recovery improves when hard sessions are separated by easier days or rest days.

- Consistent sleep schedules support energy, recovery, and training adherence.
- Reduced sleep is a reason to scale intensity or volume down temporarily.

- If soreness lasts several days, reduce sets, intensity, or exercise novelty.
- If a user misses sessions often, simplify the schedule before adding more work.
- Deload-style weeks can be useful when fatigue accumulates across several weeks.

chunks.title: Exercise Safety and Red Flags
chunks.text:
Define retrieval content that reinforces safe boundaries instead of encouraging
the model to improvise around risk.

- Chest pain
- Fainting or near-fainting
- Sudden severe shortness of breath
- Severe dizziness
- Sharp or worsening pain during a movement

- When symptoms suggest medical risk, the plan should pause and recommend
  professional assessment.
- When a movement causes pain, reduce complexity, load, or range of motion
  instead of forcing progression.
- Beginners should not be pushed into high-skill lifts without a safer fallback.

- The knowledge base may explain when to stop or scale down.
- The knowledge base should not diagnose injuries or prescribe rehabilitation.

chunks.title: Full-Body Strength for Beginners
chunks.text:
Describe a conservative strength template for users who need simple, repeatable
exercise selection.

- Favor compound patterns that train multiple muscle groups with simple setup.
- Pick stable exercises before highly technical or explosive variations.
- When possible, pair each lower-body movement with a push and a pull movement.
- Home plans should prioritize bodyweight, dumbbells, resistance bands, and
  chair or bench-supported variations.

- Most beginner strength work fits well in 2 to 4 sets of 6 to 12 repetitions.
- Use loads that allow clean technique and controlled tempo.
- Stop sets when technique clearly degrades instead of chasing extra repetitions.
- Rest about 60 to 120 seconds between moderate strength sets.

- Add repetitions before load when the user has limited equipment.
- Add load only when the current rep range feels repeatable with solid form.
- If recovery is poor, reduce volume before removing training entirely.

chunks.title: Full-Body Strength for Beginners
chunks.text:
- If a movement irritates a joint, swap to a simpler variation in a pain-free
  range of motion.
- If equipment is unavailable, preserve the movement pattern before chasing an
  exact exercise match.
